hero.title: Writing
hero.subtitle: Using stories, reflection, and language to make sense of people, place, faith, and the world around me.

sections.text:
Like many writers, my relationship with writing began first as a relationship with reading.

As a child, I constantly rewrote the stories I encountered — changing endings, introducing new twists, or recreating them with characters and situations that felt closer to home. Long before I understood writing as a craft, I was already trying to reshape the worlds I encountered through imagination.

Through adolescence and into university, writing became a steady companion. I wrote poems, short stories, stage plays, and spoken pieces for school activities, church programmes, and personal exploration. At the time, it was mostly instinctive — something I did because I loved it.

That changed in 2015.

sections.title: Discovery
sections.text:
What transformed writing for me was discovering a community of young Ghanaian writers online who approached the craft with seriousness, intentionality, and vision. For the first time, I encountered people who believed writing could do more than entertain — that it could shape culture, preserve memory, challenge systems, and contribute meaningfully to society.

Just as importantly, there were mentors.

Writers who took younger creatives seriously. People who helped us sharpen our craft, refine our thinking, and find our voices without losing ourselves in imitation.

That season gave writing new meaning for me. What had once been private expression slowly became purpose.

sections.title: Building
sections.text:
As my interest deepened, I began writing more consistently through blogs and social media platforms, experimenting with form, voice, and storytelling.

A major turning point came when one of my short stories was published by the Ghana Writers Project. For a young writer still trying to understand his place within the literary space, that moment mattered deeply. It opened doors to workshops, conversations, radio features, and a wider creative community that helped shape my development as a writer.

Around this time, together with friends and collaborators, we started several literary and media initiatives including The FB Magazine and later 2EWEBOYS, a culture and commentary platform that eventually won Best New Blog at the 2016 Ghana Blogging Awards.

Those projects taught me far more than writing alone. They taught me editing, collaboration, audience-building, publishing, and the discipline required to consistently create meaningful work.

sections.title: Community
sections.text:
One of the most defining parts of my writing journey has been community-building.

Together with mentors, fellow writers, and the 2EWEBOYS team, we founded the Writers & Readers' Grotto — a monthly literary retreat where writers gathered to share work, receive feedback, and grow together in craft and confidence.

Through the Grotto, we organised workshops, mentorship sessions, readings, and training opportunities for emerging writers. We also launched the Kids Grotto, creating space for younger readers and writers to build confidence in public reading, storytelling, and creative expression from an early age.

One of the projects I remain most proud of from that period was our collaboration with German partners including VKII and GIZ on Cr8tive Resistance, an anthology of poetry and short fiction inspired by the Sustainable Development Goals through the eyes of young Ghanaian writers.

sections.title: Practice
sections.text:
Over the years, my writing has evolved significantly.

What began primarily as fiction, poetry, and short-form storytelling has expanded into reflective writing, cultural commentary, editorial work, mentorship, and publishing. While imagination still remains central to how I think and write, I have become increasingly interested in the relationship between stories and society — how writing shapes identity, memory, faith, leadership, and public life.

I co-edited Growing Up – An Anthology of Everyday Experiences from Everyday People and helped lead the publication of Words of the Wind. I also wrote the column Writing About Writing for the Business & Financial Times newspaper for several years, where I explored Ghana's literary landscape while sharing reflections on craft, creativity, and the writing life.

My work has also appeared in the Ama Ata Aidoo Centre for Creative Writing anthologies Adabraka and Larabanga.

Beyond writing itself, I have remained deeply invested in developing younger writers and contributing to Ghana's literary ecosystem through workshops, mentorship, editing, and creative development spaces. I have served as mentor and editor for the Goethe-Institut's Young Creative Writers Lab, helping support emerging voices through multiple editions of the programme.

sections.title: Today
sections.text:
Currently, I serve as a Partner at DAKPABLI & ASSOCIATES, one of Ghana's foremost independent publishing houses, alongside mentors and writers Kofi Akpabli and Nana Awere Damoah. Our work spans writing, editing, publishing, manuscript development, and broader literary consulting.

Alongside this, I also work independently as a freelance editor, writing consultant, and publishing professional.

For me, writing has always remained more than a profession or creative outlet.

It is the space where reflection becomes language.
Where imagination becomes understanding.
And where meaning can be built carefully, one sentence at a time.

signature: The space where reflection becomes language.
And where meaning can be built carefully, one sentence at a time.
